attributes.stem:
A 65-year-old woman presents to the nephrology clinic for routine follow-up of her chronic kidney disease. She has stage IV chronic kidney disease (estimated glomerular filtration rate 22 mL/min/1.73m2), hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her medications include perindopril 10 mg daily, amlodipine 10mg daily, linagliptin 5mg daily and monthly subcutaneous erythropoietin. She reports feeling well with no symptoms apart from mild fatigue.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. There is no peripheral oedema. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Heart sounds are dual with no murmurs. There are vesicular breath sounds throughout the chest on auscultation.

Recent blood tests show potassium 5.6 mmol/L (3.5-5.0). Her nephrologist is keen to continue perindopril given its efficacy in controlling blood pressure and proteinuria, although is concerned regarding the hyperkalaemia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which of the following medications would be most appropriate to commence in this situation, thereby allowing the continuation of perindopril?
attributes.explanation:
This clinical case represents a common scenario. Drugs such as ACE inhibitors and ARBs (angiotensin II receptor blockers) have long-term nephroprotective properties through reducing blood pressure and proteinuria. However, as renal impairment progresses through to CKD stages IV and V, serum potassium levels often increase. If hyperkalaemia is left unaddressed, it can precipitate an earlier need to commence long-term dialysis and/or life-threatening cardiac arrhythmia.

In such a scenario, options include discontinuation of the ACE inhibitor or ARB (and use of alternate agents to manage blood pressure), dose reduction of the ACE inhibitor or ARB (with close monitoring of serum potassium levels), or continuation of the ACE inhibitor or ARB with the addition of an agent such as patiromer. Patiromer is a novel agent for the management of hyperkalaemia. Agents such as resonium have been available for many years although are poorly tolerated. Patiromer is an ion exchange polymer. In the gut, patiromer exchanges potassium for calcium. By binding potassium, free concentration of potassium for absorption is reduced and excess potassium is excreted in the faeces.

Whilst furosemide and to a lesser extent, hydrochlorothiazide, can both reduce serum potassium, their use in this patient would be inappropriate given their euvolaemic clinical status, and the potential for over-diuresis to result in a further decline in renal function. Entresto (sacubitril/valsartan) and spironolactone can increase serum potassium and are hence incorrect.
learningPoints: Patiromer is a potassium binder that can be used to manage hyperkalaemia in patients who would benefit from continuation of RAAS blockade agents.
references: New drug: Patiromer sorbitex calcium for hyperkalaemia. Australian Prescriber. 2019 Dec 17;43(1):28–9. Available at: https://australianprescriber.tg.org.au/articles/patiromer-sorbitex-calcium-for-hyperkalaemia.html
